footer_brand_desc: A community dedicated to preserving and celebrating the divine legacy of Lord Vishwakarma — the eternal craftsman of the gods.
home_hero_subtitle: Deva Shilpi · Divine Architect · Patron of Craftsmen
home_hero_desc: Celebrating the eternal legacy of Lord Vishwakarma — the celestial craftsman who built Lanka, Dwarka, and Indraprastha, and forged the divine weapons of the gods. A community rooted in skill, devotion, and timeless tradition.
home_features_desc: From the mythology of Lord Vishwakarma to the living traditions of the Panch Jati — discover your roots.
home_f1_p: The divine architect of the universe — builder of celestial cities, creator of divine weapons, and patron deity of all craftsmen.
home_f2_p: Thousands of years of craftsmanship legacy — the Panch Jati clans who shaped civilizations with their hands and hearts.
home_f3_p: Vishwakarma Jayanti and Vishwakarma Puja — sacred celebrations where tools and machinery are worshipped with devotion.
home_f4_p: The Vishwakarma community spans across India — united by faith, profession, and an unbreakable bond with their divine ancestor.
home_f5_p: Lohars, Sutrdhars, Tameras, Shilpis, and Daivagyas — five clans, five crafts, one divine lineage tracing back to Lord Vishwakarma's sons.
home_f6_p: Reach out for community events, information, or to connect with fellow members of the Vishwakarma Parivar across India.
home_about_p1: Lord Vishwakarma (विश्वकर्मा) is the celestial engineer and master craftsman of the gods in Hindu mythology. His name means "All-Maker" in Sanskrit — reflecting his supreme creative power over the entire universe.
home_about_p2: Mentioned in the Rigveda, the Vishnu Purana, the Mahabharata, and the Ramayana, he is the divine architect responsible for building the legendary golden city of Lanka, the sunken city of Dwarka, and the magnificent Indraprastha for the Pandavas.
home_about_p3: He is also the creator of divine weapons — Indra's Vajra, Vishnu's Sudarshana Chakra, Shiva's Trishula — and the mythical flying machine Pushpaka Vimana.
home_festival_p: Every year on 17th September (Kanya Sankranti), artisans, engineers, factory workers, and craftsmen across India worship their tools and machines in honour of Lord Vishwakarma — seeking his blessings for skill, safety, and prosperity.
home_quote_text: Vishvakarma is all-seeing — he who has on all sides eyes, on all sides a face, on all sides arms, on all sides feet, who, when producing heaven and earth, shapes them with his arms and his wings.
lv_hero_desc: The celestial architect, divine craftsman, and creator of wonders — the supreme engineer of the universe.
lv_bio_p1: Lord Vishwakarma (Sanskrit: विश्वकर्मा, meaning "All-Maker") is the divine architect and engineer of the gods in Hindu mythology. He is the patron deity of all craftsmen, artisans, engineers, architects, and workers — revered as the supreme creative force behind all physical construction in both the mortal and divine worlds.
lv_bio_p2: In the Rigveda, two full hymns (Mandala 10, Hymns 81 and 82) are dedicated to Vishvakarma, describing him as the all-seeing god who shapes heaven and earth, who has eyes, faces, arms, and feet on all sides. He is described as the omniscient architect of the universe itself.
lv_bio_highlight: Etymology: The name Vishwakarma comes from the Sanskrit words Vishwa (universe/all) and Karma (creator/maker) — literally meaning "Creator of the Universe" or "All-Maker."
lv_bio_p3: In the Vishnu Purana, Vishwakarma is identified as the son of Prabhasa — one of the eight Vasus — and his wife Varastri, the sister of Brihaspati (the preceptor of the gods). This places him firmly within the highest divine lineage of Vedic cosmology.
lv_bio_p4: The early Vedic tradition also used "Vishvakarma" as an honorific epithet for any supremely powerful deity, particularly one associated with creative power. Over time, the name crystallised into a specific deity — the divine craftsman and architect worshipped especially by artisan communities across India.
lv_bio_p5: Lord Vishwakarma is traditionally depicted as a wise, dignified figure with four arms and a long white beard — symbolising his age, wisdom, and mastery. He is adorned with a crown and gold jewellery, befitting his divine status. His four hands hold:
lv_bio_li2: A book (Vedas) — representing divine knowledge and design
lv_bio_li3: A noose — representing the binding of creation
lv_bio_li4: Craftsman's tools — hammer, chisel, or other instruments
lv_bio_p6: He rides or is accompanied by his vahana (vehicle), the Hamsa — a swan or goose — symbolising wisdom, discernment, and the ability to separate the pure from the impure.
lv_bio_p7: Lord Vishwakarma had five sons, each of whom became the ancestor of one of the five great craftsman clans that form the Vishwakarma community — collectively known as the Panch Jati:
lv_bio_son1: Manu — married Kanchna, daughter of Sage Angira; ancestor of the ironsmiths (Lohars)
lv_bio_son2: Maya — married Soumya, daughter of Sage Parasar; ancestor of the carpenters (Sutrdhars)
lv_bio_son3: Twashta — married Jayanti, daughter of Sage Kaushik; ancestor of the coppersmiths (Tameras)
lv_bio_son4: Shilpi — married Karuna, daughter of Sage Bhrigu; ancestor of the sculptors (Shilpakars)
lv_bio_son5: Daivagya — married Chandrika, daughter of Sage Jaimini; ancestor of the goldsmiths (Daivagyas)
lv_creations_desc: From celestial cities to flying machines — Lord Vishwakarma's creations shaped the very fabric of Hindu mythology.
lv_c1_p: Built originally as a divine abode, the golden city of Lanka was constructed by Vishwakarma on order of Lord Shiva. It later became the kingdom of Ravana, described in the Ramayana.
lv_c2_p: The magnificent underwater city of Dwarka was built by Vishwakarma for Lord Krishna and the Yadavas. After Krishna's departure from earth, the city submerged into the sea.
lv_c3_p: The grand capital of the Pandavas, built upon request from Lord Krishna and the Pandavas. Its magnificent halls and palace of illusions were so wondrous that even the Kauravas were bewildered.
lv_c4_p: The world's first flying vehicle — an aerial chariot of extraordinary beauty, originally built for Kubera and later taken by Ravana. It could expand to any size and fly at will.
lv_c5_p: Vishwakarma crafted the magnificent chariot of Surya (the Sun God), drawn by seven horses representing the seven colours of light. He also trimmed Surya's excessive radiance to make it bearable for mortals.
lv_c6_p: Lord Vishwakarma carved the original wooden idols of Lord Jagannath, Balabhadra, and Subhadra at the famous Jagannath Temple in Puri, Odisha — one of the four sacred dhams of India.
lv_c7_p: The great city of Hastinapur — capital of the Kuru kingdom and the main setting of the Mahabharata — was also constructed by the divine architect Vishwakarma.
lv_c8_p: Vishwakarma designed and built Swarga Loka (the celestial heaven) — the magnificent abode of the gods, with its palaces, gardens, and divine architecture beyond mortal imagination.
lv_weapons_desc: The greatest weapons in Hindu mythology were not found — they were crafted by the master craftsman of the gods.
lv_w1_p: The most powerful weapon in Indra's arsenal — forged from the bones of the sage Dadhichi who willingly sacrificed himself. The Vajra is indestructible and unstoppable.
lv_w2_p: The spinning disc weapon of Lord Vishnu, capable of cutting through anything in the universe. Forged from the radiance of the Sun God himself, it represents the cosmic wheel of time.
lv_w3_p: The iconic three-pronged weapon of Lord Shiva — representing creation, preservation, and destruction. It is the most recognisable symbol of Shiva's supreme power.
lv_w4_p: The divine mace of Lord Vishnu, also crafted by Vishwakarma. It is said to be capable of destroying any being in the three worlds and symbolises the power of knowledge.
lv_w5_p: Every divine chariot used in the celestial wars and battles was designed and constructed by Vishwakarma — including those of Indra, Surya, and the other principal deities.
lv_w6_p: Various divine missile weapons (astras) used in the great epics were crafted or empowered by Vishwakarma, making him the ultimate armourer of the celestial realm.
lv_quote_text: He who is our father, our creator, the disposer, who knows all worlds, who alone gives names to the gods — to him all other beings come with their questions. Vishvakarma is abundant in wisdom.
her_hero_desc: Thousands of years of craftsmanship, devotion, and tradition — the story of the Vishwakarma community across the ages.
her_intro_h2: A Civilisation Built by Our Hands
her_intro_p1: The Vishwakarma community is one of the oldest artisan communities in India, tracing their divine lineage directly to Lord Vishwakarma — the celestial architect of the universe. Their ancestors built the temples, forged the tools, carved the sculptures, and cast the metals that shaped Indian civilisation.
her_intro_p2: From the ancient Vedic period through the great empires — Maurya, Gupta, Chola, Vijayanagara, and Mughal — the craftsmen of the Vishwakarma community built the monuments, sacred spaces, and everyday objects that define India's cultural and artistic heritage.
her_intro_p3: Today, members of the community are engineers, architects, jewellers, sculptors, carpenters, and blacksmiths — continuing a tradition of excellence in craftsmanship that spans millennia.
her_highlight: The Vishwakarma community is also known as Viswabrahmins in South India, Panchal in Western India, and Vishwakarmis across the rest of India.
her_panch_desc: Lord Vishwakarma had five sons — each the ancestor of one of the five great craftsman clans that together form the Vishwakarma community. Each clan specialises in a sacred craft passed down through generations.
her_clan1_spouse: Married Kanchna, daughter of Sage Angira
her_clan2_spouse: Married Soumya, daughter of Sage Parasar
her_clan3_spouse: Married Jayanti, daughter of Sage Kaushik
her_clan4_spouse: Married Karuna, daughter of Sage Bhrigu
her_clan5_spouse: Married Chandrika, daughter of Sage Jaimini
her_gotras: 125 Sub-Clans (Gotras): Each of the five clans is further divided into 25 sub-groups (gotras), giving a total of 125 distinct ancestral lineages within the Vishwakarma community.
her_timeline_desc: From the Vedic age to modern times — the enduring contribution of the Vishwakarma community to Indian civilisation.
her_t1_p: Two complete hymns in the Rigveda (Mandala 10, Hymns 81–82) are dedicated to Vishvakarma, establishing him as the all-seeing cosmic creator and divine architect.
her_t2_p: Vishwakarma builds Lanka for Ravana, Dwarka for Krishna, and Indraprastha for the Pandavas — the master architect of the great epics.
her_t3_p: Vishwakarma craftsmen build the great Hindu temples across India — the intricate Dravidian and Nagara styles that still stand today in Ellora, Mahabalipuram, and Badami.
her_t4_p: Artisans of the Vishwakarma community produce masterpieces of stone sculpture, bronze casting, wood carving, and metalwork that define classical Indian art.
her_t5_p: Craftsmen serve the Vijayanagara, Maratha, and Rajput kingdoms as master builders, sculptors, and weapon-smiths — their work shaping the forts and palaces of medieval India.
her_t6_p: Despite colonial disruption, Vishwakarma artisans preserve ancient crafts. Communities organise around trade guilds and temple associations to maintain their cultural identity.
her_t7_p: Members of the Vishwakarma community become engineers, architects, jewellers, and industrialists — contributing to India's post-independence industrial and technological growth.
her_t8_p: The Vishwakarma community continues its proud tradition of craftsmanship while embracing modern technology — from traditional hand tools to advanced engineering and digital fabrication.
her_contrib_desc: The Vishwakarma community's hands have shaped India's greatest monuments, tools, art, and sacred spaces.
her_contrib1_p: The intricate gopurams, shikhars, mandapams, and stone carvings of India's thousands of temples were built by Vishwakarma craftsmen following the ancient Shilpa Shastra texts.
her_contrib2_p: From the intricate filigree of Odisha to the kundan work of Rajasthan, the Daivagya clan has produced India's most celebrated jewellery traditions across centuries.
her_contrib3_p: Agricultural tools, weapons, household implements — the Lohar clan's mastery of iron and metal has supported Indian agriculture and defence since ancient times.
her_contrib4_p: The elaborate wooden architecture of Kerala temples, Rajasthani havelis, and the chariots of temple festivals are testimony to the Sutrdhars' unmatched woodworking skill.
her_contrib5_p: The Shilpi clan brought stone to life — from the sculptures of Khajuraho to the serene Buddha at Sarnath, their chisel shaped India's greatest artistic masterpieces.
her_contrib6_p: Today, the community's tradition of precision and craftsmanship lives on in thousands of engineers, architects, and industrial workers who build modern India.
her_quote_text: The craftsman who builds well, carves truly, and creates with devotion — he follows in the divine footsteps of Vishwakarma, the maker of all things seen and unseen.
fes_hero_desc: Honouring the divine craftsman — the festivals where tools become sacred, work becomes worship, and devotion meets creation.
fes_main_desc: Two major festivals celebrate the divine architect — Vishwakarma Jayanti and Vishwakarma Puja — each with its own tradition, date, and regional significance.
fes_f1_p1: Vishwakarma Puja is the most widely observed celebration of Lord Vishwakarma, observed every year on 17th September — the day when the Sun transits into the Virgo (Kanya) zodiac sign, known as Kanya Sankranti. On this day, craftsmen, factory workers, engineers, artisans, and all who work with tools and machinery offer prayers to Lord Vishwakarma.
fes_f1_p2: Workshops, factories, garages, construction sites, and manufacturing units across India observe this day as a sacred holiday. Tools, machinery, vehicles, and equipment are cleaned, decorated with flowers, and worshipped in a formal puja ceremony before any work resumes.
fes_f1_ci2: Factories and workshops close for puja
fes_f1_ci6: Paper kite flying in some regions
fes_f2_p1: Vishwakarma Jayanti celebrates the birth anniversary of Lord Vishwakarma. This festival falls on Bhadra Purnima — the full moon day in the Hindu month of Bhadra, which typically falls in August or September. Many Vishwakarma community members consider this the more sacred of the two festivals.
fes_f2_p2: On this day, special prayers, havan (fire rituals), and community processions are organised. In some regions, elaborate idols of Lord Vishwakarma are installed, worshipped for the day, and then ceremonially immersed (visarjan) in rivers or water bodies.
fes_f3_p1: In some parts of India — particularly West Bengal, Bihar, and Assam — Vishwakarma Puja is also observed the day after Diwali, on Govardhan Puja or Padwa day.
fes_f3_p2: In South India, the Vishwakarma community (known as Viswabrahmins) observes the Ayudha Puja on Vijayadasami (Dussehra) — a pan-Indian tradition of worshipping tools, weapons, and instruments of one's trade.
fes_how_desc: A step-by-step guide to performing the traditional Vishwakarma Puja with devotion and reverence.
fes_step1_p: On the day before or morning of the puja, all tools, machines, vehicles, and equipment are thoroughly cleaned. Workshops and workplaces are decorated with marigold garlands, mango leaves, and colourful rangoli.
fes_step2_p: A picture or idol of Lord Vishwakarma is placed on a decorated platform (chowki). The tools and instruments are arranged before the deity for blessing. Fresh flowers, incense, and oil lamps are arranged.
fes_step3_p: The puja begins with the priest or senior community member taking a sankalpa (sacred vow) and invoking Lord Vishwakarma with Vedic mantras. The gathering joins in devotional songs (bhajans).
fes_step4_p: A sacred fire (havan kund) is lit. Offerings of ghee, sesame, and sacred herbs are made while chanting Vishwakarma mantras. The fire is believed to carry prayers directly to the deity.
fes_step5_p: Each tool, machine, and instrument of the trade is touched to the feet of the deity, anointed with turmeric and kumkum, and blessed. This is the central act of the puja — honouring the instruments of livelihood.
fes_step6_p: The puja concludes with the distribution of prasad (blessed food) to all present. Many communities organise a large communal feast (langar) where people of all backgrounds are welcomed to celebrate together.
fes_sig1_p: The puja is a moment of deep gratitude — thanking Lord Vishwakarma for the skills, tools, and work that sustain the livelihood of craftsmen, workers, and engineers throughout the year.
fes_sig2_p: Workers in hazardous environments — factories, construction sites, mines — seek Lord Vishwakarma's blessings for protection from accidents and safe working conditions in the year ahead.
fes_sig3_p: Vishwakarma Puja brings together the entire community — irrespective of their specific clan or craft — in a shared celebration that reinforces communal bonds and collective identity.
fes_sig4_p: The festival embodies the principle that skilled work is a form of divine worship. Every nail driven, every sculpture carved, every circuit designed is an act of devotion to Vishwakarma.
fes_sig5_p: Vishwakarma Puja is observed not just by the Vishwakarma community but by factory workers, mechanics, and artisans across India — making it one of the most widely observed occupational festivals.
fes_sig6_p: In a rapidly modernising world, Vishwakarma Puja keeps ancient traditions alive — connecting modern engineers and factory workers with thousands of years of sacred craftsmanship heritage.
fes_quote_text: On this sacred day, every tool becomes a weapon of creation, every workplace a temple, and every craftsman a devotee of Vishwakarma — the eternal maker of all things.
abt_hero_desc: A united family — bound by faith, tradition, and the eternal legacy of Lord Vishwakarma, the master craftsman of the universe.
abt_who_p1: Vishwakarma Parivar is the community of descendants of Lord Vishwakarma — the divine architect of the universe in Hindu tradition. We are a community united not merely by ancestry, but by a shared faith, a common cultural heritage, and a deep reverence for the art of creation.
abt_who_p2: Our community is known by different names across India — Vishwakarma, Viswabrahmins, Panchal, Vishwakarmis — but our identity is singular: we are the children of the divine maker, the Panch Jati — the five sacred clans of craftsmen, artists, engineers, and builders.
abt_who_p3: This website is our community's digital home — a space to preserve our history, share our traditions, connect our members, and introduce the world to the magnificent legacy of Lord Vishwakarma.
abt_m1_p: To document, preserve, and celebrate the cultural, religious, and artisanal traditions of the Vishwakarma community for future generations — so that our ancient legacy is never forgotten.
abt_m2_p: To bring together the millions of Vishwakarma community members across India and the world — creating a platform for connection, mutual support, and collective celebration of our shared identity.
abt_m3_p: To inspire the next generation with the story of Lord Vishwakarma — showing that the pursuit of craft, skill, and creation is not just a profession but a sacred calling rooted in divine tradition.
abt_values_desc: The values that have guided the Vishwakarma community through thousands of years of history.
abt_v1_p: From Lord Vishwakarma's divine creations to the humblest village blacksmith — we believe that every act of creation deserves the fullest dedication and the highest skill.
abt_v2_p: Our work is our worship. We carry forward the tradition that skilled craftsmanship is a spiritual practice — every creation an offering to Lord Vishwakarma and the divine.
abt_v3_p: The five clans of the Panch Jati are one family. We support one another through shared traditions, collective celebrations, and mutual care across generations.
abt_v4_p: Following Lord Vishwakarma who held the Vedas in his hand — we value education, the passing of knowledge from master to apprentice, and the pursuit of both ancient wisdom and modern learning.
abt_v5_p: Like the divine craftsman who built palaces for others and served the gods selflessly, we believe in humble service — building not for personal glory but for the good of all.
abt_v6_p: We honour the ancient while embracing the future. Our traditions are not frozen in time — they evolve, adapt, and grow, just as the craftsman's tools change with the age.
abt_presence_desc: The Vishwakarma community spans across every state of India, known by different regional names but united by the same divine lineage.
abt_r1_p: Known as Vishwakarma or Panchals. Predominantly ironsmiths (Lohars) and carpenters (Sutrdhars) in states like Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Haryana, and Punjab.
abt_r2_p: Known as Viswabrahmins in Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala. The community is highly respected as temple builders and bronze casters.
abt_r3_p: In Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, and Assam, the community is known as Vishwakarma. They are famous for the Durga Puja idol-making tradition and the Jagannath temple connection in Puri.
abt_r4_p: In Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Goa, members of the community are known as Panchal or Vishwakarma and are prominent goldsmiths, carpenters, and bronze workers.
abt_r5_p: In Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh, the Vishwakarma community includes skilled ironsmiths, bell-metal casters, and woodworkers who supply tools to farming communities across the region.
abt_r6_p: Vishwakarma community members have settled across the world — in the UK, USA, Canada, Australia, and Southeast Asia — carrying their traditions, faith, and festivals with them wherever they go.
abt_cta_h2: Be Part of the Vishwakarma Parivar
abt_cta_p: Whether you are a member of the Vishwakarma community or someone who wishes to learn about our traditions — we welcome you. Reach out to connect, contribute, or simply say Jai Vishwakarma!
abt_quote_text: We are the children of the divine maker — our hands shape the world, our faith sustains it, and our devotion to Lord Vishwakarma guides every stroke of the chisel, every blow of the hammer.
con_hero_desc: We'd love to hear from you — whether you're a community member, a researcher, or simply curious about Lord Vishwakarma and our traditions.
con_reach_p: Have a question about our community, upcoming events, or Lord Vishwakarma? We're here to help. Reach out through any of the channels below.
con_hours_p: Monday to Saturday, 10:00 AM – 6:00 PM IST
con_wa_p: Join our community group for updates on events and celebrations.
con_form_desc: Fill in the form below and we'll get back to you within 2 business days. All messages are responded to personally.
con_ph_message: Tell us what's on your mind — a question, a story, or just a greeting. Jai Vishwakarma!
con_form_note: Your details are kept private and will never be shared with third parties.
con_faq1_a: Vishwakarma Puja is celebrated every year on 17th September — the day of Kanya Sankranti when the Sun transits into the Virgo zodiac. Vishwakarma Jayanti (Birthday) falls on Bhadra Purnima, typically in August/September.
con_faq2_q: What are the five clans of the Vishwakarma community?
con_faq2_a: The five clans (Panch Jati) are: Lohars (ironsmiths, descendants of Manu), Sutrdhars (carpenters, descendants of Maya), Tameras (coppersmiths, descendants of Twashta), Shilpis (sculptors, descendants of Shilpi), and Daivagyas (goldsmiths, descendants of Visvajna).
con_faq3_q: How can I join the Vishwakarma Parivar community?
con_faq3_a: Use the contact form above or email us at info@vishwakarmaparivar.org. We welcome all members of the Vishwakarma community and friends who wish to learn about our traditions and participate in our celebrations.
con_faq4_q: Is Lord Vishwakarma worshipped only by craftsmen?
con_faq4_a: While Lord Vishwakarma is the patron deity of craftsmen, engineers, and artisans, his worship has spread to all who work with their hands or minds in creative and productive work. Factory workers, architects, mechanics, and engineers of all communities observe Vishwakarma Puja across India.
con_faq5_q: Where is Lord Vishwakarma mentioned in the scriptures?
con_faq5_a: Lord Vishwakarma is mentioned in the Rigveda (Mandala 10, Hymns 81–82), the Vishnu Purana, the Ramayana (as builder of Lanka and Pushpaka Vimana), and the Mahabharata (as builder of Dwarka and Indraprastha).
con_quote_text: Jai Vishwakarma — may the blessings of the divine architect guide your hands, inspire your mind, and fill your work with the grace of the eternal maker.
